cta_spec.purpose: Production CTA specification for comparison-style websites with homepage, filters, product listing/table, and affiliate actions.
global_principles: Each page must have one primary user goal.
Each section should guide users toward one dominant next action.
CTA labels must describe the outcome of the action, not the system process.
Primary CTAs must be visually dominant within their section.
Secondary CTAs must support the primary CTA without competing with it.
Tertiary CTAs must remain visible but visually quiet.
CTA wording must be clear, literal, concise, and action-oriented.
Do not use vague labels such as 'Click Here', 'Continue', 'More', or 'Learn More' without context.
CTA hierarchy must remain consistent across all pages.
CTAs must reflect user intent and funnel stage.
Low-commitment CTAs should be used earlier in the funnel.
High-commitment CTAs should appear only when sufficient trust and context exist.

discovery.description: User is understanding what the site does.

evaluation.description: User is filtering, comparing, and reviewing options.

decision.description: User is ready to visit an external store or complete a meaningful next step.

primary.purpose: Main next step the user should take in a page or section.
usage_rules: Only one primary CTA should dominate per section.
A page may contain multiple primary CTAs only if they are scoped to separate sections and do not compete visually within the same viewport.
Primary CTA should use the strongest button treatment.
Primary CTA copy should usually be 2 to 4 words.

secondary.purpose: Supporting action that helps users without distracting from the main goal.
usage_rules: Secondary CTA must be visibly less dominant than primary.
Secondary CTA may appear next to primary when both actions are truly complementary.
Secondary CTA should not use the same emphasis level as primary.

tertiary.purpose: Low-emphasis utility action or optional interaction.
usage_rules: Tertiary CTA should appear as ghost, text, or icon-with-label where appropriate.
Tertiary CTA should not visually compete with primary or secondary actions.
Tertiary CTA is suitable for sort, expand, save, or auxiliary controls.

general: Start with a verb when possible.
Describe what the user gets after clicking.
Use plain language over clever language.
Prefer concise labels between 2 and 4 words.
Use title case or sentence case consistently across the product.
Avoid jargon unless the audience clearly understands it.

avoid: pressure language unless intentionally used for urgency modules

visual_hierarchy: Primary CTA must be the most prominent interactive element in its section.
CTA emphasis must be created through color, contrast, size, spacing, and position.
Do not give low-priority actions the same visual weight as primary actions.

sm.usage: tight controls, compact rows, utility buttons

md.usage: default button size for most interfaces

lg.usage: hero sections, major calls-to-action, touch-friendly layouts

shape.border_radius: Use a consistent radius across all CTA variants; do not mix multiple unrelated radii.

spacing: Keep at least 8px between adjacent CTAs.
Use 12px to 16px gaps when grouping primary and secondary actions.
Provide enough whitespace around primary CTA so it remains visually isolated.
icon_usage: Icons may support CTA meaning but should not replace text for important actions.
Use arrow icons only when they clearly reinforce forward navigation.
Avoid icon-only primary CTAs unless the pattern is universally recognized and the context is obvious.

rules: All CTA buttons must have visible feedback on hover or press.
Keyboard focus must be clearly visible.
Disabled buttons must appear non-interactive and should not create confusion about what is missing.
State changes must remain consistent across all CTA variants.

global: Place CTAs only after the user has enough context to act confidently.
Do not bury the main CTA below unrelated content.
Place the primary CTA near the end of a logical information block.
Keep CTA alignment predictable within similar sections.
hero: Hero CTA should appear above the fold when the page has a clear top-level action.
Hero CTA should follow headline and supporting value text.
Use one primary CTA and optionally one secondary CTA in the hero.
filters: Filter CTA should appear at the end of filter controls or in a sticky action zone if the filter panel is long.
Apply and Reset actions must be visually distinguishable.
Do not make Reset visually stronger than Apply.
listing_or_table: Per-item CTA must be attached directly to the relevant row or card.
Comparison and details actions must be clearly separated.
Affiliate CTA must not be visually confused with internal product-detail actions.
empty_state: Empty-state CTA should help users recover quickly.
Use recovery-oriented copy such as 'Clear Filters' or 'Browse All Printers'.

homepage.page_goal: Help users immediately understand the site and begin browsing or comparing printers.

rules: Use only one primary hero CTA.
Primary hero CTA should be low-friction and discovery-oriented.
Do not use a purchase CTA as the main homepage hero action unless the site is commerce-first.

secondary_cta.placement: next to or below hero primary CTA
rules: Secondary hero CTA must support exploration, not compete with the primary CTA.
Avoid more than one secondary CTA in the hero.

filter_section.section_goal: Help users narrow the product list with confidence.

rules: There must be one clear action to commit filter changes.
If filtering is live and updates instantly, remove the apply CTA and instead show clear state feedback.
Reset/Clear must never be more visually dominant than Apply.
Reset/Clear should be easy to find but visually safer.

product_table_or_listing.section_goal: Help users inspect, compare, and move into decision-making.

rules: Use internal CTA for deeper product research.
Keep labels consistent across all rows/cards.
Use tertiary treatment for utility actions.
Avoid cluttering each row with too many low-value actions.

affiliate_actions.section_goal: Send ready users to external stores with clear intent.

primary_cta.placement: row/card action area after user sees enough product context
rules: Affiliate CTA must be visually distinct from internal detail actions.
Use outcome-focused language that matches user intent.
Do not disguise affiliate CTA as internal navigation.

supporting_note_rules: If needed, indicate that the action leads to an external store.
Use subtle disclosure if affiliate relationships need to be transparent.

empty_state.state_goal: Help the user recover from zero results or incomplete searches.

rules: The CTA must help the user recover quickly.
Do not leave the empty state without a suggested next action.

support_text_rules: Briefly explain why no results were found.
Suggest the most likely way to recover.

rules: Stats bar should not contain dominant CTAs.
Stats are informational and should support trust, not compete for action.
Quick filter chips are interaction shortcuts, not primary CTAs.
Selected state must be obvious.
Sort and view toggles are utility controls and should not use primary CTA styling.

anti_patterns: Multiple competing primary CTAs in the same section
Using the same strong visual treatment for every button
Generic CTA text with unclear outcome
Primary CTA hidden below dense content
Affiliate CTA indistinguishable from internal navigation
Reset action styled like the main action
Too many per-row CTAs causing decision fatigue
Icon-only primary actions without clear text labels
CTA copy that promises more than the click delivers
accessibility_rules: CTA text must meet minimum color contrast requirements.
Buttons must be large enough for touch interaction.
Focus states must be visible for keyboard users.
Button labels must remain understandable to screen readers.
Icon buttons must have accessible labels.

notes: Track CTA clicks by section and placement.
Track click-through rate separately for internal versus affiliate CTAs.
Measure whether CTA wording changes impact downstream conversions.

implementation_notes_for_ai: Do not invent new CTA labels unless they follow the copy rules in this spec.
Do not style utility controls as primary actions.
Keep CTA hierarchy stable across all pages and components.
When uncertain, choose clarity over creativity.
If a section lacks a clear user goal, simplify the section before adding more CTAs.
